title: Backend API Endpoints
description: A curated view of the current FOM backend API surface and the main endpoint groups that already exist.
content:
## Conventions

* Base URL: `/api/v1`
* Authentication: bearer access token
* Pagination: `limit` and `cursor`
* Time-sensitive filters: shop-local timezone
* Response shape: standardized success and error envelopes

## Docs and testing surfaces

* Swagger UI: `/docs`
* OpenAPI JSON: `/openapi.json`
* OpenAPI YAML: `/openapi.yaml`
* Scalar reference: `/reference`

## Main endpoint groups

### Public marketing

* `GET /public/plans`

### Authentication and profile

* register, login, token refresh, logout
* email verification and password reset
* social login and phone OTP flows
* `GET /users/me`
* `PATCH /users/me`

### Notifications and messaging

* inbox notifications and unread counts
* mark-as-read and mark-all-read operations
* category-level notification preferences
* email provider-backed delivery workflows

### Realtime and push

* websocket ticket issuance
* push-device registration and deactivation

### Platform workspace

* dashboard metrics
* shops, users, subscriptions, invoices, and support issues
* settings for profile and plan catalog management

### Shop operations

* shops and membership management
* billing visibility
* customers
* orders and order items
* Messenger parsing for suggested order drafts
* message templates
* deliveries
* summaries, reporting, and exports

## Feature-gated areas

Some endpoint groups are explicitly tied to stable plan feature codes.

* customer operations require `customers.management`
* order operations require `orders.management`
* Messenger parsing additionally requires `orders.parse_messenger`
* template management requires `templates.management`
* deliveries require `deliveries.management`
* reporting depends on `reports.analytics`
* member management depends on `team.members`
* export routes depend on `exports.csv`

## Why this matters for integrations

The current backend surface already exposes the system boundaries that future partner APIs will
need:

* auth and identity
* tenant and shop scoping
* order lifecycle data
* customer and delivery resources
* notifications and realtime events
* plan-aware access enforcement

title: API & Integrations
description: The current backend API reference and the future direction for third-party integrations on the FOM Platform.
content:
This section serves two purposes:

* document the current backend API surface
* frame the future API needed for third-party integrations

## Current state

Today, the platform already has a documented backend surface with:

* authenticated operational routes
* shop and platform workspaces
* exports, notifications, realtime tickets, and push-device registration
* public plan catalog endpoints
* documented OpenAPI and Scalar reference URLs

## Future plan

Third-party integration is still a forward-looking capability, but the groundwork is visible.

* response envelopes are already standardized
* resource boundaries are documented
* feature gating and access control are explicit
* API-related docs live beside the broader product and platform reference

<Note type="warning" title="Availability">
  The API documentation here is useful for planning and internal implementation now, but it should
  not be treated as a public partner API commitment yet.
</Note>

title: Messenger Integration
description: Set up Meta Messenger OAuth, webhook delivery, and sender profile enrichment for the FOM inbox, order parsing, and auto reply flow.
content:
This guide matches the current FOM implementation used by the shop owner portal inbox.

## What FOM does today

* shop owner clicks `Connect Facebook Page` in the inbox page
* FOM starts Meta OAuth automatically
* if Meta returns one Page, FOM connects it immediately
* if Meta returns multiple Pages, FOM shows one selection screen
* inbound messages are stored in the inbox
* message threads can be used for order drafting and auto reply rules
* FOM tries to enrich sender names and locale through the Messenger user profile flow

## URLs used by this repo

These are the two URLs you must configure in Meta.

* OAuth redirect URI:
  `https://<your-web-app-domain>/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback`
* Messenger webhook callback URL:
  `https://<your-api-domain>/api/v1/messenger/webhooks/meta`

In local code, those come from:

* web route: [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback/route.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback/route.ts)
* API webhook controller: [apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts)
* API config builder: [apps/api/src/config/app-config.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/config/app-config.service.ts)

## Required env vars

Fill these before testing the integration.

```env
# apps/api/.env
PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL="https://api.example.com"
WEB_APP_BASE_URL="https://app.example.com"
META_APP_ID=""
META_APP_SECRET=""
META_LOGIN_CONFIG_ID=""
META_WEBHOOK_VERIFY_TOKEN=""
META_GRAPH_API_BASE_URL="https://graph.facebook.com"
META_GRAPH_API_VERSION="v25.0"
MESSENGER_TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_SECRET=""
```

The web app should also point to the deployed API and public app domain:

```env
# apps/web/.env
API_BASE_URL="https://api.example.com"
NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL="https://api.example.com"
NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL="https://app.example.com"
WEB_APP_BASE_URL="https://app.example.com"
```

## Meta app setup

### 1. Create the correct app type

Create a **Business** type Meta app. FOM uses **Facebook Login for Business**, not the legacy manual token flow.

Official doc:

* [Facebook Login for Business](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/facebook-login/facebook-login-for-business)

### 2. Add the right products

Add these products in the Meta App Dashboard:

* `Facebook Login for Business`
* `Messenger`
* `Webhooks`

### 3. Create a Facebook Login for Business configuration

Inside `Facebook Login for Business`:

1. open `Configurations`
2. create a configuration
3. choose **User access token**
4. select the Page permissions needed by FOM
5. copy the generated `Configuration ID`

Put that configuration ID into `META_LOGIN_CONFIG_ID`.

The current FOM flow is started here:

* [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/route.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/route.ts)

The API exchanges the authorization code here:

* [apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts)

### 4. Add the permissions FOM needs

For the current inbox, reply, and page discovery flow, use this as the practical minimum:

* `pages_show_list`
* `pages_manage_metadata`
* `pages_read_engagement`
* `pages_messaging`

For sender name enrichment, request the Meta feature used for Messenger profile lookup:

* `Business Asset User Profile Access`

Why this matters in FOM:

* page discovery uses `me/accounts`
* replies use the Page access token through the Send API
* webhook subscription depends on Page metadata access
* sender profile enrichment is performed after connect and during inbound ingestion

Relevant implementation:

* page discovery and code exchange: [apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts)
* profile enrichment and backfill: [apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts)

Official docs:

* [Facebook Login for Business](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/facebook-login/facebook-login-for-business)
* [Messenger User Profile API](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/identity/user-profile)

### 5. Register the OAuth redirect URI

Add this exact redirect URI in the Meta app login settings:

```text
https://<your-web-app-domain>/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback
```

If this does not match the running web domain, the user will fail on the Meta return step.

### 6. Configure the webhook

Use the API webhook endpoint:

```text
https://<your-api-domain>/api/v1/messenger/webhooks/meta
```

Set the verify token to the same value as `META_WEBHOOK_VERIFY_TOKEN`.

FOM verifies the challenge and validates signatures here:

* challenge verification: [apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts)
* webhook controller: [apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts)

For the current implementation, subscribe the Page to the message events FOM actually handles:

* `messages`
* `messaging_postbacks`

Official docs:

* [Messenger webhook setup](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/getting-started/webhook-setup)
* [Messenger webhooks](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/webhooks)

### 7. Put the app in the right mode

Use development mode only for testers, app roles, and owned assets.

Before real customer onboarding:

* complete App Review where required
* move the app to live mode
* confirm the production domains are whitelisted
* test with a Page not owned only by the developer account

## Local development

### Why localhost does not work for webhook subscription

Meta validates the webhook by making a server-to-server request to your callback URL. `localhost`,
`127.0.0.1`, and private LAN addresses are not reachable from Meta's servers.

That means the webhook URL must be:

* publicly reachable
* HTTPS
* mapped to your local app through a tunnel or a deployed dev environment

This is an implementation inference from the webhook verification flow: Meta has to reach your
callback URL directly in order to verify the challenge and later deliver POST events.

### Recommended local setup

Use one public tunnel for the web app and one for the API, or deploy a shared dev environment.

Example:

* web tunnel:
  `https://fom-web-dev.ngrok-free.app`
* API tunnel:
  `https://fom-api-dev.ngrok-free.app`

Then update:

* `WEB_APP_BASE_URL`
* `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL`
* `PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL`
* `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL`

And register these tunnel URLs in Meta:

* OAuth redirect URI:
  `https://fom-web-dev.ngrok-free.app/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback`
* webhook callback URL:
  `https://fom-api-dev.ngrok-free.app/api/v1/messenger/webhooks/meta`

## End-to-end test checklist

1. Set the env vars in API and web.
2. Start API and web on the domains you configured.
3. Open `/dashboard/inbox`.
4. Click `Connect Facebook Page`.
5. Complete the Meta permission screen.
6. If multiple Pages are returned, choose one.
7. Confirm the inbox overview shows the connected Page.
8. Send a real Messenger message to that Page.
9. Confirm a thread appears in FOM.
10. Test reply sending from the thread detail page.
11. Confirm sender name enrichment is visible if profile access is approved.

## Troubleshooting

### OAuth starts but Meta rejects the login

Check:

* app type is `Business`
* `META_LOGIN_CONFIG_ID` is valid
* the exact callback URL is whitelisted
* requested permissions are included in the selected configuration

### Connect succeeds but no messages arrive

Check:

* webhook URL is public and HTTPS
* `META_WEBHOOK_VERIFY_TOKEN` matches the Meta dashboard value
* the Page is subscribed to the correct webhook fields
* the API domain in `PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL` is the same one configured in Meta

### Messages arrive but sender name stays as PSID

Check:

* `Business Asset User Profile Access` is approved
* the Page access token is valid
* profile enrichment is not blocked by missing permissions or a restricted test asset

### Reply sending fails

Check:

* the Page selected during connect is the correct one
* the stored Page token still has the required scopes
* the app is live and approved for the assets you are trying to use

## FOM flow reference

* inbox overview: [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/page.tsx](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/page.tsx)
* connect start: [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/route.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/route.ts)
* connect callback: [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback/route.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/callback/route.ts)
* page selection: [apps/web/app/(shop-admin)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/select/page.tsx](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/web/app/\(shop-admin\)/dashboard/inbox/connect-meta/select/page.tsx)
* backend integration service: [apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/messenger.service.ts)
* webhook controller: [apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts](/Users/mixin/Projects/FacebookOrderManager/fom-platform/apps/api/src/messenger/meta-messenger-webhook.controller.ts)

## Official references

* [Facebook Login for Business](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/facebook-login/facebook-login-for-business)
* [Messenger app setup](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/getting-started/app-setup)
* [Messenger webhook setup](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/getting-started/webhook-setup)
* [Messenger webhooks](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/webhooks)
* [Messenger User Profile API](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/messenger-platform/identity/user-profile)

title: Response Envelope

_searchMeta.cleanContent: standard rest api response structure goals consistent response shape for all endpoints easy client parsing for success and errors clear error codes for localization common headers x-request-id: unique request identifier content-type: application json success 200 201 list response with pagination 200 empty list 200 validation error 422 authentication error 401 forbidden 403 not found 404 conflict 409 rate limited 429 server error 500 recommended error codes code http when--- --- ---validation _error 422 input validation failedunauthorized 401 no or invalid tokenforbidden 403 user lacks permissionnot _found 404 resource missingconflict 409 invalid state transitionrate _limited 429 too many requestsserver _error 500 unhandled error notes for laravel use a global exception handler to map exceptions to the standard format return request_id from middleware for tracing keep message user-friendly and use code for client logic
keywords:
## Recommended Error Codes

| Code | HTTP | When |
| --- | --- | --- |
| VALIDATION\_ERROR | 422 | Input validation failed |
| UNAUTHORIZED | 401 | No or invalid token |
| FORBIDDEN | 403 | User lacks permission |
| NOT\_FOUND | 404 | Resource missing |
| CONFLICT | 409 | Invalid state transition |
| RATE\_LIMITED | 429 | Too many requests |
| SERVER\_ERROR | 500 | Unhandled error |

## Notes for Laravel

* Use a global exception handler to map exceptions to the standard format
* Return

title: Getting Started
description: Start here to understand what FOM Docs covers, who it serves, and how the documentation is organized.
content:
This documentation site is the new home for the FOM Platform knowledge base.

It is organized around three needs:

## What is available today

* A docs-first UI built from the Vercel `Documents` starter structure.
* Searchable markdown and MDX content.
* A seeded `User Manual` section for operational guidance.
* Reference pages pulled from the platform's existing documentation set.

## How to use this site

<Note type="warning" title="Current scope">
  Third-party integration APIs are still a future plan. The API section currently documents the
  backend surface and the response conventions that will support that future work.
</Note>

title: Architecture
description: The current application and deployment shape of the FOM Platform monorepo.
content:
## High-level architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Docs["Docs App (Next.js)"] --> Repo["Monorepo"]
  Web["Web App (Next.js)"] --> Repo
  Mobile["Mobile App (Flutter)"] --> API["API (NestJS)"]
  Web --> API
  API --> DB[("PostgreSQL")]
  API --> Cache["Redis / background workflows"]
  API --> Mail["Email + notification adapters"]
```

## Current monorepo shape

| Area | Technology | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `apps/api` | NestJS | Auth, shops, orders, billing, notifications, exports, and platform APIs |
| `apps/web` | Next.js | Marketing, platform-owner workspace, shop-owner workspace |
| `apps/mobile` | Flutter | Mobile operational workflow for active shops |
| `apps/docs` | Next.js | Product, operator, and API documentation |
| `packages/*` | Shared configs and UI | Reusable TypeScript, ESLint, and UI utilities |

## Design choices

* The workspace is organized as a Turborepo so builds, linting, and type checks stay package-based.
* The docs app is now a standalone Next.js site that uses MDX content and static search data.
* The API is the enforcement layer for permissions and plan-gated features.
* Web and mobile clients consume the same core backend surface while presenting different
  operational experiences.

## Why this matters

The documentation site is no longer a single hardcoded page. It now mirrors the platform structure
closely enough to explain:

* how operator workflows map to backend capabilities
* where future third-party integration work fits
* how the different applications in the monorepo relate to one another

  Web --> API
  API --> DB[("PostgreSQL")]

  API --> Mail["Email + notification adapters"]

## Current monorepo shape

| --- | --- | --- |
|

| NestJS | Auth, shops, orders, billing, notifications, exports, and platform APIs |
|

| Next.js | Marketing, platform-owner workspace, shop-owner workspace |
|

title: Feature Set
description: The current platform feature areas that shape the docs and user guidance.
content:
## Current feature areas

### Core operations

* order creation and status progression
* customer management
* delivery coordination
* template management
* summaries and reporting

### Platform capabilities

* subscription-aware access control
* stable plan feature codes for runtime enforcement
* CSV export across shop and platform workflows
* inbox notifications and realtime invalidation
* web and mobile surfaces for operators

### Access model

The platform uses both RBAC permissions and plan-gated feature codes.

* backend guards remain the source of truth for protected actions
* client surfaces can still make informed access decisions using JWT claims and plan visibility
* billing and recovery flows remain reachable even when operational access is restricted

## Documentation implications

The docs site needs to explain more than UI behavior.

* some features depend on shop subscription state
* some features are available only to specific roles
* some capabilities already exist internally before they become public integration commitments

title: Product Overview
description: A current summary of what the FOM Platform ships today and how the product is positioned.
content:
## Summary

FOM Order Manager is an operational platform for Facebook-first shops.

The current platform focuses on the workflows that matter most for day-to-day shop execution:

* orders
* customers
* deliveries
* message templates
* summaries and reports
* exports
* notifications
* billing visibility
* RBAC-backed team access

## Product position

The platform is intentionally narrower than a full ERP.

* It is designed for fast operational use instead of broad back-office complexity.
* It keeps manual workflows where that improves speed and clarity for local teams.
* It already includes the foundations for subscription-aware feature access and future
  integrations.

## Current application surface

The monorepo currently contains four main applications:

| Application | Role |
| --- | --- |
| `apps/api` | NestJS backend for auth, RBAC, subscriptions, exports, notifications, and operational APIs |
| `apps/web` | Marketing site plus platform-owner and shop-owner web workspaces |
| `apps/mobile` | Flutter mobile app for shop-owner operations |
| `apps/docs` | This documentation site |

## Operational principles

* fast entry and status visibility matter more than broad automation
* feature access must be enforceable at runtime, not only described in UI copy
* the platform should support both internal operations and future external-facing API work

title: Roadmap
content:
# Roadmap

## Phase 0: MVP

Timeframe: 5 to 7 days

* Manual order entry
* Order list and filters
* Status updates
* Daily summary
* Burmese UI

## Phase 1: Early Feedback

Timeframe: 2 to 4 weeks

* Fix friction points in order entry
* Improve list speed and filtering
* Onboarding and tips in Burmese

## Phase 2: Version 2

Timeframe: 1 to 2 months

* Delivery tracking
* Message templates
* Weekly and monthly reports
* Multi-user staff accounts

## Phase 3: Growth

Timeframe: 3 months

* Subscription billing
* Customer segmentation
* CSV import

title: Security & Privacy
content:
# Security and Privacy

## Access Control

* Shop data is scoped by `shop_id`
* Owner role has full access
* Staff role limited to orders and delivery updates

## Data Protection

* Encrypt data in transit using HTTPS
* Use database encryption at rest from the hosting provider
* Avoid storing sensitive data beyond phone and address

## Privacy Practices

* Provide consent text for storing customer phone numbers
* Allow deletion of customer records
* Allow export of orders for the shop owner

## Audit and Monitoring

* Record status changes in order\_status\_events
* Store audit logs for key changes
* Alert on repeated failed login attempts

## Backups and Retention

* Daily backups for core collections
* Archive completed orders after 90 days
* Retain audit logs for at least 6 months

title: Customers, Deliveries & Exports
description: How customer management, delivery handling, and export workflows fit into the current FOM experience.
content:
## Customers

Customer management stays lightweight and operational.

* Customer records are tied closely to order history.
* Search and segmentation are intended to support fast lookup rather than CRM-heavy workflows.
* Per-shop customer write access is already enforced through plan features and permissions.

## Deliveries

The delivery flow is structured for assignment and status visibility.

* Delivery routes sit alongside orders instead of acting as a separate disconnected tool.
* Driver assignment and delivery progression are designed to feed back into the order workflow.
* Current documentation already covers the delivery API surface and the shop-scoped access rules.

## Exports

CSV export is already part of the platform story across API, web, and mobile surfaces.

* Operational exports help owners save records without leaving the current workflow.
* Shop exports are plan-aware, so actions can be disabled when the active subscription does not
  allow them.
* Platform exports extend the same pattern to internal administrative use cases.

title: User Manual
description: Operator guidance for the current FOM workflow across orders, customers, deliveries, roles, and billing.
content:
Use this section when the goal is operating the product, not implementing the platform.

## Who this section is for

* Shop owners managing orders and staff activity.
* Managers reviewing deliveries, exports, and billing state.
* Team members who need a quick understanding of the daily workflow.

## Current coverage

## Product stance

FOM is designed around Facebook-first shops that need a reliable operational system before they
need complex automation.

* Manual input still matters because it keeps the workflow fast and locally understandable.
* Daily visibility matters more than broad ERP scope.
* Access control and plan gating are already part of the product model, even while the platform is
  still expanding.

title: Orders & Daily Workflow
description: The current operational loop for entering orders, updating statuses, and reviewing summaries in FOM.
content:
## Daily operating loop

The current product is optimized around a short, repeatable loop:

## What the product currently supports

* Manual order entry remains the primary operating path.
* List filtering is central to the workflow, especially for `today`, `pending`, and delivered work.
* Status updates are built to reduce friction for high-volume daily operations.
* The reporting layer is focused on operational visibility, not deep accounting.

## Related platform behavior

* Order APIs are plan-gated through `orders.management`.
* Messenger parsing already exists as a documented backend capability for a suggested order draft.
* Notifications and realtime invalidation are designed to keep the web and mobile surfaces current
  when order state changes.

title: Team Access & Billing
description: How roles, subscription state, and plan-gated capabilities affect operational access in FOM.
content:
## Team access

The platform already models access at two levels:

* platform-scoped permissions for the internal owner workspace
* shop-scoped roles and permissions for day-to-day tenant operations

This matters in practice because operational pages, exports, and member management are not just
UI toggles. They map to stable capability checks in the backend.

## Subscription behavior

* Trialing, active, and overdue subscriptions currently keep the operational workspace available.
* Expired, cancelled, and inactive states block plan-gated features.
* Billing and recovery surfaces stay reachable so a shop can inspect status and restore access.

## Why this matters for documentation

Operator documentation needs to explain not only what a page does, but also why a user may or may
not be able to access it.

* `team.members` affects member management routes.
* export access depends on the relevant plan items.
* shop billing pages are part of the operational recovery path.
